title: Understanding GitHub
subtitle: Your code in the cloud

sections.content: So far, git has been tracking your files locally — everything lives on your computer. But what if your computer breaks? What if you want to share your code with someone? That's where **GitHub** comes in.
sections.analogy: Git is like a journal where you record every change. GitHub is like putting a copy of that journal in a safe deposit box — it's backed up, secure, and you can share it with others.
keyPoints: GitHub stores your code online as a backup
It lets others see, download, and contribute to your code
Most open-source software lives on GitHub
Git works without GitHub, but GitHub needs git

sections.question: What's the relationship between git and GitHub?
options: Git tracks changes locally, GitHub stores them online
sections.explanation: Git is the tool that tracks changes on your computer. GitHub is an online service that stores your git repositories in the cloud. You can use git without GitHub, but GitHub relies on git.

sections.content: Three key commands connect your local git to GitHub. These are the bridge between your computer and the cloud.
keyPoints: `git push` — upload your commits to GitHub
`git pull` — download new commits from GitHub
`git clone` — download an entire project from GitHub for the first time

sections.question: You want to download someone else's project from GitHub to your computer. Which command do you use?
sections.explanation: `git clone` downloads an entire project and its full history from GitHub. You use it when getting a project for the first time. After that, `git pull` gets any new updates.

completionMessage: You now understand the relationship between git and GitHub. Git tracks changes locally, GitHub stores them online. Next, you'll learn how to push your code to GitHub.
